theory.intro: A misleading graph shows correct data but is drawn so it gives a false impression. To explain why one misleads, name the exact feature that distorts the picture and say what wrong impression it creates.
facts: A **truncated axis** (vertical axis not starting at $0$) makes small differences look large.
An **irregular or missing scale** — uneven steps, unequal time gaps, or no numbers — hides the true size of a change.
**Unequal bar widths** or an **enlarged pictograph symbol** exaggerate a value by area, not just height.
**Cherry-picked or missing data** mislead — for example percentages that add to more than $100\%$ cannot be shares of one whole.
A **fair** graph starts its scale at $0$, uses equal steps and equal widths, and shows all the data.
steps: Check the vertical axis starts at $0$ and rises in equal steps.
Check the categories have equal bar widths or equal symbol sizes.
Check all the data is shown and any percentages add to $100\%$.
Name the feature that distorts the picture and the false impression it gives.

foundation.solution_text: The vertical axis starts at $5000$, not $0$. Cutting off the bottom makes Belford's bar look several times taller than Ashby's, even though $5680$ is only a little more than $5280$.
Shop B's bar is drawn much wider than Shop A's. The extra width makes Shop B look far bigger by area, even though it sold only slightly more ($550$ against $500$).
The three percentages are $60\%$, $55\%$ and $45\%$, which add to $160\%$. Shares of one whole must total $100\%$, so these figures cannot all be shares of the same set of A grades.
The vertical axis has no scale or numbers. Without a scale you cannot read the actual sales for any month, so the size of the increase cannot be judged.
No. The vertical axis starts at $0$ and rises in equal steps, and the bars have equal widths. The bars are almost the same height, which fairly shows that the three populations are close.

development.solution_text: The 2021 value ($200$) is double the 2020 value ($100$), but the symbol is doubled in both width and height. That makes its area four times as large, so the increase looks far bigger than doubling.

The years $2014$, $2015$ and $2019$ are drawn with equal gaps, but the last gap is four years, not one. Spacing unequal time gaps equally makes the rate of increase look steady when it actually slowed after $2015$.

Faith sees that Class B's bar towers over the rest. The graph is misleading because the axis starts at $71.7$, not $0$. Every class averaged about $72$, so the real differences are only tenths of a mark; the cut-off axis blows them up.

Graph P is misleading. Its vertical axis starts at $5000$, not $0$, so Belford's bar looks several times taller than the others. Graph Q starts at $0$, so the bars are almost equal, fairly showing that the three populations are close.

mastery.solution_text: First, the vertical axis starts at $30$, not $0$, so the small gap between $40$ and $44$ is stretched. Second, Store B's bar is drawn much wider than Store A's, so its larger area exaggerates the difference. Both make Store B look far ahead of a close result.

Belford's bar looks about twice Ashby's only because the axis starts at $5000$, not $0$. In real numbers $5680$ is just $400$ more than $5280$ — about $8\%$ more, nowhere near double. The truncated axis, not the data, creates that impression.
